All Christians are dedicated and devoted to Christ through baptism, and they have a strict obligation to be holy. However, the true church of God includes everyone who is sanctified in Christ Jesus, called to be saints, and who call upon him as God manifest in human form for all the blessings of salvation; those who acknowledge and obey him as their Lord, and as Lord of all. It includes no other people. Christians are distinguished from nonbelievers and atheists because they dare not live without prayer; and they are distinguished from Jews and pagans because they call on the name of Christ. Notice how often in these verses the apostle repeats the words, Our Lord Jesus Christ. He was not afraid to mention him frequently or honorably. To all who called upon Christ, the apostle gave his usual greeting, desiring for them God’s pardoning mercy, sanctifying grace, and comforting peace through Jesus Christ. Sinners cannot have peace with God, nor any from him, but through Christ. He gives thanks for their conversion to the faith of Christ; that grace was given to them by Jesus Christ. They had been enriched by him with all spiritual gifts. He speaks of expression and knowledge. And when God has given these two gifts, he has given great power for usefulness. These were gifts of the Holy Spirit, by which God bore witness to the apostles. Those who wait for the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ will be kept by him until the end; and those who are so will be blameless on that day, made so by rich and free grace. How glorious are the hopes of such a privilege: to be kept by the power of Christ from the power of our corruptions and Satan’s temptations!

In matters of religion, be of one mind; and where there is not unity of sentiment, still let there be union of affection. Agreement in the greater things should extinguish divisions about the lesser. There will be perfect union in heaven, and as we approach it on earth, we come closer to perfection. Paul and Apollos were both faithful ministers of Jesus Christ, and helpers of their faith and joy; but those disposed to be contentious broke into parties. So liable are the best things to be corrupted, and the gospel and its institutions made engines of discord and contention. Satan has always tried to stir up strife among Christians, as one of his chief strategies against the gospel. The apostle left it to other ministers to baptize while he preached the gospel, as a more useful work.

Paul had been raised in Jewish learning; but the simple preaching of a crucified Jesus was more powerful than all the eloquence and philosophy of the heathen world. This is the essence of the gospel. Christ crucified is the foundation of all our hopes, the source of all our joys. And through his death we live. The preaching of salvation for lost sinners by the sufferings and death of the Son of God appears foolish to those on their way to destruction, if it is explained and faithfully applied. The sensual, the covetous, the proud, and ambitious alike see that the gospel opposes their favorite pursuits. But those who receive the gospel and are enlightened by the Spirit of God see more of God’s wisdom and power in the doctrine of Christ crucified than in all his other works. God left a great part of the world to follow the dictates of human reason, and experience has shown that human wisdom is folly and unable to find or retain knowledge of God as Creator. It pleased him, by the foolishness of preaching, to save those who believe. By the foolishness of preaching; not by what could justly be called foolish preaching. But the thing preached was foolishness to worldly-wise men. The gospel has always been, and ever will be, foolishness to all on the road to destruction. The message of Christ, plainly delivered, has always been a sure touchstone by which people can learn what path they are traveling. But the despised doctrine of salvation by faith in a crucified Savior, God in human nature, purchasing the church with his own blood to save multitudes—even all who believe—from ignorance, delusion, and vice—has been blessed in every age. And the weakest instruments God uses are stronger in their effects than the strongest men can use. Not that there is foolishness or weakness in God, but what people consider as such overcomes all their admired wisdom and strength.

God did not choose philosophers, nor orators, nor statesmen, nor men of wealth, power, and influence in the world to publish the gospel of grace and peace. He best judges what men and what methods serve his purposes. Though not many wealthy people are usually called by Divine grace, there have been some such in every age who have not been ashamed of the gospel of Christ; and people of every rank need pardoning grace. Often, a humble Christian, though poor in this world, has more true knowledge of the gospel than those who have made the letter of Scripture the study of their lives, but who have studied it rather as the witness of men than as the word of God. And even young children have gained such knowledge of Divine truth as to silence skeptics. The reason is, they are taught by God; the design is that no human being should boast in his presence. That distinction, in which alone they might boast, was not of themselves. It was by God’s sovereign choice and regenerating grace that they were in Jesus Christ through faith. He is made from God to us wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption—all we need or could desire. And he is made wisdom to us so that, through his word and Spirit, and from his fullness and treasures of wisdom and knowledge, we may receive all that will make us wise for salvation and fit for every service to which we are called. We are guilty, liable to just punishment; and he is made righteousness, our great atonement and sacrifice. We are depraved and corrupt, and he is made sanctification so that in the end he may be made complete redemption—may free the soul from the being of sin and loose the body from the bonds of the grave. And this is so that all flesh, according to the prophecy by Jeremiah, may glory in the special favor, all-sufficient grace, and precious salvation of Jehovah.
